title: Chapter 1 Quiz
description: Test your knowledge of the Introduction to TypeScript!

questions.question: What is TypeScript?
options: A superset of JavaScript that adds static typing.
A completely unrelated language to JavaScript.
questions.explanation: TypeScript builds on top of JavaScript by adding optional static typing.

questions.question: Every valid JavaScript file is also a valid TypeScript file.
questions.explanation: Because TS is a superset of JS, it accepts all standard JS code.

questions.question: Can Google Chrome natively understand and execute a `.ts` file?
options: Yes, natively out of the box.
Only if you install a browser extension.
No, it must be compiled down to JavaScript first.
questions.explanation: Browsers only run JavaScript, so the TS code must be compiled first.

questions.question: What is the process of converting TypeScript code into plain JavaScript called?
questions.explanation: The TypeScript compiler transpiles TS code into JS code.

questions.question: The abbreviation for the TypeScript compiler is _____.
questions.explanation: The CLI command to run the TypeScript compiler is `tsc`.

questions.question: Which of these is a major benefit of using TypeScript?
options: It makes web pages load faster over the network.
It automatically styles your HTML components.
It catches type-related errors before the code runs.
It gives you free cloud hosting.
questions.explanation: TS catches bugs at compile-time when you write the code, instead of run-time in the browser.

questions.question: Which symbol is used to perform a 'type annotation' in TypeScript?
questions.explanation: A colon is used. Example: `let name: string = 'Alice';`

questions.question: If you don't provide a type annotation, TypeScript assumes the variable cannot be used.
questions.explanation: TypeScript will try to 'infer' the type based on the initial value you provide.

questions.question: If you write `let message = 'Hello';`, what type does TypeScript infer for `message`?
questions.explanation: TS sees the string value 'Hello' and infers that `message` is of type `string`.

questions.question: What happens if a variable has no type annotation and no initial value?
questions.explanation: Without a type and a value, TS doesn't know what it is, so it assigns the `any` type, which turns off checking.

questions.question: TypeScript forces you to drop JavaScript and learn entirely new syntax for loops and functions.
questions.explanation: No! It is just regular JavaScript with some extra type features added on top.

questions.question: What is the expected file extension for a standard TypeScript file?
questions.explanation: Standard TypeScript files use the `.ts` extension.

questions.question: Is `let count: number = 10;` a valid TypeScript line?
options: Yes, it explicitly sets the type to number.
No, you cannot specify types manually.
No, there shouldn't be a colon.
questions.explanation: Yes, `let count: number = 10;` is the standard way to annotate and assign a variable in TS.

questions.question: What will TypeScript do on the following code snippet?\n`let score = 50;`\n`score = 'winner';`
options: It will compile perfectly without warnings.
It will convert 'winner' into the number 0.
It will throw a compile-time error because 'score' was inferred as a number.
It will crash the user's browser immediately.
questions.explanation: Because `score` was initialized as 50, TS infers it as a number. Changing it to a string later causes a compile error.

questions.question: TypeScript gives you better autocomplete (IntelliSense) in editors like VS Code.
questions.explanation: Because TS knows the shape of your data, the editor can accurately predict properties and methods as you type!

questions.question: If you don't explicitly declare a type, TypeScript will use type ______ to guess the variable's type from its assigned value.
questions.explanation: Type inference is when TS automatically deduces the type.

questions.question: Which of these statements perfectly sums up TypeScript?
questions.explanation: TypeScript is exactly that: JavaScript with static typing!

questions.question: What company initially created and maintains TypeScript?
questions.explanation: TypeScript was developed and is maintained by Microsoft.

questions.question: Type annotations act as a form of up-to-date documentation for other developers reading your code.
questions.explanation: By explicitly marking parameters as strings or numbers, future developers easily understand how to use your functions.

questions.question: Which of these variable definitions is fully recommended to ensure type safety if a value is not assigned immediately?
questions.explanation: If you don't assign a value initially, you must provide a type annotation (like `: string`) so TS doesn't default to `any`.
